content.text: Based on the official Claude Code documentation,
"What Is the Agent SDK"
refers to
the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK)

— a development library that exposes Claude Code's core capabilities as programmable tools, enabling you to build production AI agents that autonomously read files, run commands, search the web, edit code, and perform complex development workflows.

attrs.alt: What Is the Agent SDK diagram

content.text: Core Definition
The
Agent SDK is Claude Code as a library

Claude Code (CLI) → Agent SDK (library)
    ↓
Same tools, agent loop, and context management
    ↓
Programmable in Python and TypeScript
Key renaming
: "The Claude Code SDK has been renamed to the Claude Agent SDK".
What It Enables
AI Agent Capabilities
Build AI agents that can:
Capability
Description
Read files
Access and analyze codebase files
Run commands
Execute shell commands, scripts
Search the web
Browse for information, research
Edit code
Modify source files, create new code
Autonomous workflows
Build their own context, decide trajectories, work autonomously
What it does
: "Give you the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, programmable in Python and TypeScript".
Key Features
1. Built-in Tools

# Agent SDK includes built-in tools for:
- reading files
- running commands
- editing code
Benefit
: "Your agent can start working immediately without you implementing tool execution".
2. The Agent Loop (Core Architecture)
Four-step loop

1. Gather Context → Agent reads files, runs commands to understand situation
2. Take Action → Agent decides what to do (edit code, run commands)
3. Verify Work → Agent checks if action succeeded
4. Repeat → Loop until task complete
Definition
: "Agents are systems that build their own context, decide their own trajectories, and work very autonomously".
3. Harness (Agent Infrastructure)
A robust agent requires more than just a model — it needs a
Harness
containing:
Component
Purpose
Tools
Bash tool, file operations, command execution
Prompts
System instructions, guidance
File System
Context engineering, state management
Skills
Pre-built capabilities, commands
Sub-agents
Delegate to other agents
Memory
Files as memory, documentation
[youtube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TqC1qOfiVcQ)
4. Opinionated Design
The SDK bakes in lessons from deploying Claude Code
Opinion
Why
Bash tool is most powerful
General computer use > bespoke tools
Files as memory
Agent writes to files to "remember" things
Context Engineering
File system for state management, not just prompt engineering
Key insight

: "Instead of building custom tools for every action (e.g., API wrapper for file conversion), giving the agent access to shell allows it to use existing software (ffmpeg, grep, git) to solve problems flexibly, similar to how a human developer works".
Programming Languages
Language
Version Requirement
Python
Python 3.10+
TypeScript
Node.js 18+
[github](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python)
Use Cases
When the Agent SDK is Beneficial
Scenario
Why SDK Works
Precise tool call management
Require precise control over sequence of tool calls
CI without human involvement
Implement Continuous Integration autonomously
Integration into larger systems
Agent within existing system with state management
Multi-agent orchestration
Delegate between agents, custom storage
Example
: "You can supply it with specifications, and it will execute a series of skills or commands autonomously, ultimately generating a pull request once the task is completed".
What It's NOT
The Agent SDK is
not
Not What
Why
AI model
Doesn't decide what agent says
Orchestration engine
You wire in AI service or business logic
No-code builder
Requires programming in Python/TypeScript
Clarification
: "The Agent SDK doesn't decide what an agent says. These elements are the job of whatever AI service or business logic the developer wires into the agent".
Mental Model
Think of the Agent SDK like
turning a power tool into a programmable robot

: Claude Code is the power drill (brilliant, autonomous, does everything). The Agent SDK is the mechanical arm + controller that lets you program the drill to do specific tasks automatically — read this file, run this command, edit this code, verify it worked, repeat until done. You're not building a new drill; you're exposing the existing drill's capabilities to your code.
Key Takeaways
Agent SDK = Claude Code as a library (formerly Claude Code SDK, renamed)
Build AI agents that autonomously read files, run commands, search web, edit code
Same tools, agent loop, context management as Claude Code — programmable in Python/TypeScript
Built-in tools: reading files, running commands, editing code — no need to implement tool execution
Agent Loop: Gather Context → Take Action → Verify Work → Repeat
Harness: Tools + Prompts + File System + Skills + Sub-agents + Memory
Opinionated design: Bash tool most powerful, files as memory, context engineering via filesystem
She's not an AI model, orchestration engine, or no-code builder — you wire in your AI/service logic
Python 3.10+, TypeScript/Node.js 18+
Beneficial when: precise tool call control, CI without humans, agent in larger system, multi-agent orchestration

The SDK is built directly on top of Claude Code because Anthropic found they were "rebuilding the same parts over and over again" for internal tools, exposing Claude Code's core capabilities as a library.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of what is the agent sdk helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with what is the agent sdk has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying what is the agent sdk in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Advanced Best Practices
Production experience with what is the agent sdk has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.

Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.

Security Considerations
Deploying what is the agent sdk in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.

Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of What Is the Agent SDK in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in What Is the Agent SDK helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Performance and Optimization
Optimizing What Is the Agent SDK for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Factor
Impact
Optimization Strategy
Latency
High
Use caching and streaming responses
Throughput
Medium
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Cost
High
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Reliability
Critical
Add retries with exponential backoff
Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing What Is the Agent SDK in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Summary

This section covered What Is the Agent SDK in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.
Core Architecture of the Agent SDK

The Agent SDK wraps the raw Messages API with a stateful execution loop that handles the entire tool-use cycle automatically. When you call the Messages API directly, you receive a response that may contain tool_use blocks, and you must manually extract those blocks, invoke the corresponding functions, collect results, and re-submit everything in a follow-up API call. The Agent SDK abstracts away this plumbing entirely: you define tools as Python functions decorated with type hints, pass them to the SDK, and the SDK manages the full conversation loop until the model reaches a natural stopping point. Under the hood, the SDK maintains a mutable message list, appends tool results as user messages, and retries the model call as many times as needed until stop_reason equals end_turn or max_tokens is hit. This design keeps application code focused on tool logic rather than protocol mechanics.
Stateful message list: the SDK appends each model response and tool result to a running conversation automatically, preserving the full context window across multiple model turns.
Built-in tool dispatch: the SDK matches tool_use blocks by name, calls the registered Python function with the arguments Claude provided, and captures the return value or any raised exception.
Event streaming: the SDK exposes callbacks for on_text, on_tool_call, and on_tool_result, letting you stream output to the user before the loop completes.
Lifecycle hooks: before_tool_call and after_tool_result hooks allow logging, authorization checks, or transformations without modifying tool implementations.
Interruption and resumption: you can pause the loop after a specific tool call, inspect state, then resume — critical for human-in-the-loop approvals.
Error propagation: tool exceptions are caught, formatted as tool_result blocks with is_error=true, and sent back to the model, which can then recover or report the failure.
SDK vs Raw API Calls

The choice between the raw Anthropic SDK (client.messages.create) and the Agent SDK depends on how complex your tool-use loop is. The raw SDK is the right choice when you make a single API call with no tool use, need complete control over every message in the conversation, or are building a stateless request-response endpoint. The Agent SDK becomes valuable the moment you have tools: it eliminates the while-loop boilerplate, handles errors from tool functions gracefully, and provides a clean interface for adding capabilities without changing orchestration code. Critically, the Agent SDK does not add latency or extra API calls beyond what the agentic loop requires — it is a thin layer over the same HTTP requests you would make manually.
Use raw client.messages.create for single-turn completions with no tool calls, or when you need to inspect raw response objects for debugging.
Use the Agent SDK when you have two or more tools and want the loop managed for you — every additional tool multiplies the orchestration complexity of the raw approach.
The Agent SDK adds automatic tool dispatch, conversation state tracking, lifecycle hooks, and interruption/resumption support on top of the raw API.
Both approaches use the same underlying API endpoints, token counting, and pricing — the SDK choice affects developer experience, not cost.
For production systems, the Agent SDK reduces error surface area by centralizing retry logic and tool-result formatting in tested library code rather than application code.
Key Concepts: Agents, Tools, and Runners

Understanding the three core abstractions — Agent, Tool, and Runner — is essential before writing any Agent SDK code. An Agent is a configured model instance: it combines a Claude model identifier, a system prompt defining its persona and constraints, and a list of Tools it is allowed to use. The Agent object is stateless; it stores configuration, not conversation history. A Tool is a callable function registered with an Agent, described by a JSON schema so Claude can understand when and how to invoke it. The Runner is the execution engine: it accepts an Agent and a user message, then drives the agentic loop until a terminal condition is reached, returning the final result. This separation of concerns means you can define Tools once and attach them to multiple Agents with different system prompts, or swap the underlying model on an Agent without touching any Tool code.
Agent: a named configuration bundle (model + system_prompt + tools) — immutable at runtime; create a new Agent to change its configuration.
Tool: a Python function with a type-annotated signature; the SDK automatically generates a JSON schema from the annotations for Claude to reason about.
Runner: the stateful executor; it owns the message list, drives API calls, dispatches tools, and emits lifecycle events.
Tool result: the Python return value from a tool function, serialized to a string and sent back to Claude as a tool_result content block.
Stop condition: the Runner terminates when Claude returns stop_reason=end_turn, the token budget is exhausted, or a maximum iteration count is reached.
Context: some implementations pass a shared context object to each tool call, allowing tools to share database connections, authentication tokens, or request-scoped state.
The Agentic Loop Explained Step by Step

The agentic loop is the core execution pattern of every Agent SDK workflow. Understanding exactly what happens at each step prevents surprises when debugging live systems. The loop begins when the Runner sends the initial user message to Claude along with the tool definitions and system prompt. Claude responds with a content array that may contain text blocks (narration or thinking visible to the user), tool_use blocks (structured requests to call specific functions), or both. The Runner inspects the stop_reason: if it is tool_use, the loop continues; if it is end_turn or max_tokens, the loop terminates. For each tool_use block, the Runner calls the corresponding Python function and collects the result. All tool results are assembled into a single user message with role=user and content=[{type: tool_result, ...}], which is appended to the conversation and sent back to Claude. This continues until no tool_use blocks appear in the response.
Step 1: Runner sends messages=[{role: user, content: initial_message}] plus tool definitions to Claude.
Step 2: Claude returns a response with stop_reason and a content array possibly containing tool_use blocks.
Step 3: Runner appends the assistant response to the message list and checks stop_reason.
Step 4: For each tool_use block, Runner invokes the matching Python function with the arguments Claude provided.
Step 5: Runner assembles all tool results into a new user message and appends it to the conversation.
Step 6: Runner makes another API call with the updated message list and repeats from Step 2.
Step 7: When stop_reason=end_turn with no tool_use blocks, Runner extracts the final text response and returns it.
Installation and Initial Setup

Setting up the Agent SDK takes five minutes when your environment is configured correctly. The SDK is distributed as part of the anthropic Python package and requires Python 3.8 or later. Install it using pip in a virtual environment to avoid dependency conflicts with other projects. Your Anthropic API key must be set as an environment variable named ANTHROPIC_API_KEY — the SDK reads this automatically and raises an AuthenticationError if it is missing or invalid. For development, store the key in a .env file and load it with python-dotenv before importing the SDK. For production, use a secrets manager such as AWS Secrets Manager, Google Secret Manager, or HashiCorp Vault rather than environment variables baked into deployment artifacts.
Install: pip install anthropic in an activated virtual environment (python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate).
Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable; the SDK raises anthropic.AuthenticationError if it is absent or malformed.
For development, create a .env file with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... and load it with python-dotenv at application startup.
Verify installation: python -c "import anthropic; print(anthropic.__version__)" — should print 0.25.0 or later.
Confirm API access: run the minimal agent below and check that it returns a coherent response without HTTP errors.
For production, set rate limit retry behavior: client = anthropic.Anthropic(max_retries=3) to handle transient 529 errors.
